title: Welcome to Psychiatry: Mind, Brain, Person, Society

objectives.statement: Reconstruct the six-lens map of mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system and explain the question each lens contributes.
Explain why a one-lens account can be incomplete without assuming that every lens contributes equally in every situation.
Classify a proposed task as academic or simulated learning versus an action requiring clinical authority, and justify the boundary.

scenes.title: A field larger than one explanation
scenes.contentHtml:
A field larger than one explanation

A fictional student named Noor has started sleeping badly. She misses two seminars, struggles to concentrate, and says the room she moved into never feels quiet. One explanation points to the brain. Another points to stress. Another points to night work, money, relationships, or the university's rules.

Which explanation belongs to psychiatry?

Potentially all of them—but not as interchangeable answers, and not all with equal weight. Psychiatry becomes more accurate when it asks what each level can explain, what evidence would support it, and what it leaves open. This field is medical, but its subject cannot be reduced to an organ, a symptom list, or a conversation. Contemporary training frameworks also reach into public mental health, culture, research, systems, and the knowledge of people who use services.

That breadth is what makes psychiatry demanding and worth studying. You will move between mind and brain, individual lives and relationships, culture and institutions. In the title, “society” names the broad social terrain that the relationship, culture, and system lenses separate into more precise questions. Your job is not to make every story agree. Your job is to ask sharper questions, recognize what each answer can and cannot establish, and keep your account open to correction.

What you will be able to do

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • reconstruct six lenses—mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system—and explain the question each adds;
  • explain why using several lenses does not mean declaring six equal causes;
  • decide whether a task belongs to academic or simulated learning, or instead requires clinical authority.

You need no prior psychiatry study. No personal disclosure is required. Every example is fictional, and you may answer in writing, by speech-to-text, in a transcribed recording, or with a tutor.

Check
id check-starting-map

Before reading on, write two short answers:

  1. What do you think psychiatry studies?
  2. If you can explain a clinical action, does that mean you are authorized to perform it with a real person? Why or why not?

Keep both answers. You will revise them at the end; this is a starting point, not a score.

Callout
id callout-course-boundary

This course supports academic explanation, evidence appraisal, fictional-case reasoning, and simulation. It does not confer a medical degree, supervised patient-care experience, licensure, board eligibility, the protected title of psychiatrist, or authority to assess or treat a real person.

Source Note
claims claim-integrated-field-model, claim-training-breadth, claim-psychiatry-medical-specialty, claim-authority-requires-governed-practicesources source-psychiatry-syllabus, source-wpa-training, source-abpn-certification

The course syllabus supplies the six-part academic map and its explicit boundary. The WPA position statement supports the breadth of contemporary psychiatric training, while the ABPN page provides one current national example of psychiatry as a medical specialty with a separate certification route. These sources do not imply that one jurisdiction's route applies everywhere.

scenes.title: Six lenses, six kinds of question
scenes.contentHtml:
Six lenses, six kinds of question

A lens is a disciplined way to ask a question. It directs attention, but it does not decide the answer in advance. The six lenses in this course help you keep distinct kinds of evidence visible.

Lens Question it contributes What it must not become
Mind What is the person experiencing, remembering, expecting, feeling, or trying to do? A claim that an experience is “only in the mind.”
Brain Which bodily processes, development, sleep, substances, illness, or treatment effects should be investigated? A claim that a biological description is automatically the complete explanation.
Person What are this individual's goals, strengths, history, identity, choices, and meanings? A generic case with the person's priorities removed.
Relationship How might family, friends, groups, care relationships, support, or conflict affect what happens? An assumption that every difficulty began inside one person.
Culture How do language, shared beliefs, identity, discrimination, and social meanings shape experience and response? A stereotype applied to someone because of group membership.
System How do housing, education, work, law, services, money, and policy alter risks and possibilities? Background scenery that is noticed but never allowed to change the account.

The table is a representation of questions, not a list of six causes. A lens may reveal a strong candidate explanation, a weak one, or no relevant contribution. Several lenses can also describe different parts of one chain. For Noor, you might ask whether night work changes opportunities for sleep, whether the new room affects rest, how she interprets the change, and what her course permits when attendance falls. Those questions do not establish what is happening. They identify evidence worth seeking.

Integration without a winner-takes-all contest

Psychiatry is a medical specialty. One United States certification framework places bodily processes, psychological experience, and social context within the specialty's work. A global training statement reaches further into culture, public mental health, research, lived-experience participation, service systems, and supervised development. Together, these sources show why a serious curriculum cannot define the field as brain science alone or as conversation alone.

Integration does not mean that every explanation is compatible. Evidence may weaken or rule out a proposal. Nor does integration mean that every lens deserves equal space in every case. It means that the choice of lens is reasoned and revisable rather than automatic.

This course makes a further distinction:

  • Academic learning builds knowledge and reasoning through sources, fictional cases, analyses, and simulations.
  • Clinical authority concerns accountable actions with real people, such as assessment and treatment, within supervised and jurisdiction-specific professional arrangements.

Training arrangements vary across countries; the ABPN route is only one United States certification and licensure example. The six-lens map can travel across settings; a certification rule from one setting cannot.

Check
id check-lens-versus-cause

Noor says the room is noisy, works nights, and reports poor sleep. Which response uses the six-lens map correctly?

A. “The system lens proves that housing caused everything.”

B. “All six lenses must contribute exactly one-sixth of the explanation.”

C. “Housing and work conditions generate system-level questions; mind, brain, person, relationship, and culture remain available if evidence makes them relevant.”

Choose before reading the feedback.

Feedback. C treats the lenses as questions and leaves the weight of each explanation to evidence. A turns one useful question into a complete causal conclusion. B mistakes breadth for equal causal shares. If you chose A or B, rewrite the response using “ask whether” rather than “proves.”

Source Note
claims claim-integrated-field-model, claim-lenses-are-question-generators, claim-training-breadth, claim-psychiatry-medical-specialty, claim-routes-vary-by-jurisdictionsources source-psychiatry-syllabus, source-wpa-training, source-abpn-certification

The six-lens structure is an EmbeddedKnowledge curricular synthesis. WPA and ABPN sources support the stated breadth and the medical-specialty context. The WPA document also records international variation; the ABPN requirements are used only as a United States example. The sources do not establish that every lens is causally relevant in every situation.

scenes.title: Worked use: knowledge is not authority
scenes.contentHtml:
Worked use: knowledge is not authority

Knowing the vocabulary of a field and being authorized to act within it are different achievements. Use this four-question scope test whenever a learning task approaches real clinical action:

  1. Who is affected? A fictional case, a simulated participant, a population or policy, or a real individual?
  2. What action is proposed? Explanation and evidence appraisal, or personal assessment and treatment?
  3. What could happen if the conclusion is wrong? A revisable classroom argument, or a decision affecting someone's health, rights, or care?
  4. What governed route is required? Ordinary academic study, or supervised clinical practice and jurisdiction-specific authorization?

The test is not a licensing code. Its job is to expose why an action belongs on one side of the learning boundary.

Worked Example
id worked-example-noor-boundary

Task. Decide which proposed responses to Noor's fictional situation fit this course and explain the decisive reason.

  1. Ana maps one question from each lens and states what evidence would help distinguish the possibilities.
  2. Ben tells a real roommate with similar difficulties that the roommate has a specific disorder.
  3. Chen compares how two fictional university policies could alter sleep opportunity, privacy, access to support, and study participation.
  4. Dev recommends that a real classmate stop a medicine.

Representation. The known information is limited: Noor is fictional; Ben's roommate and Dev's classmate are real. Ana produces questions. Chen produces a policy analysis. Ben makes a personal diagnostic claim. Dev directs a treatment change. The unknowns include any real person's health information and the local professional rules.

Principle selected. Apply the four-question scope test because the difference is not the topic—every learner is discussing mental health—but the affected person, proposed action, possible consequence, and governed route.

Plan. Classify each action, justify it with at least two parts of the test, then change one detail to see whether the classification still holds.

Execution.

Learner Scope decision Reason
Ana Inside academic learning The case is fictional; the output is a revisable question map, not an assessment or treatment of a person.
Ben Requires clinical authority and adequate evidence The target is a real person, and the action assigns a diagnosis from a superficial similarity. The possible consequences extend beyond a classroom claim.
Chen Inside academic learning The objects are fictional policies; the output is a comparison of consequences and evidence needs. It does not decide care for an individual.
Dev Requires clinical authority The target is a real person, and the action directs a treatment change whose safety cannot be established from the classroom scenario.

Independent check. Change Noor from a fictional student to a real friend. You can still study the general six-lens questions without recording private details or turning the map into an assessment of the friend. You cannot use the lesson to declare what condition the friend has or what treatment the friend should change. The boundary follows the action, not the learner's confidence.

Interpretation. Academic learning can become sophisticated: you may compare evidence, analyze systems, reason through fictional cases, and practise in simulation. Those achievements can prepare someone for a separately supervised route, but they do not replace real-patient experience, competency decisions, or legal and professional authorization.

Self-explanation. Which part of the scope test—not which mental-health topic—most strongly determines why Ana and Ben fall on different sides of the boundary?

A strong answer names at least the real-versus-fictional target and the proposed action. Ben is not outside scope merely because diagnosis is “advanced.” He is outside scope because he applies a clinical conclusion to a real person without the evidence, supervision, and authority that action requires.

Source Note
claims claim-authority-requires-governed-practice, claim-routes-vary-by-jurisdictionsources source-psychiatry-syllabus, source-wpa-training, source-abpn-certification

The syllabus separates academic and simulated performance from real-patient care. The WPA statement describes supervised progression toward independent practice and international variation. ABPN supplies one current national example requiring medical training and licensure for certification. This worked example creates no universal legal rule and gives no personal clinical advice.

scenes.title: Repair shortcuts and practise the map
scenes.contentHtml:
Repair shortcuts and practise the map

A tempting compression

Misconception
id misconception-one-real-level

Two shortcuts can sound decisive:

  • “Because psychiatry is medical, the brain is the real level; relationships and systems are background.”
  • “Because social conditions matter, biology is irrelevant.”

Both fail before the evidence is even considered. Each promotes one lens into a rule that excludes the others. A brain-level account may clarify a process while leaving the person's goals or housing untouched. A system-level account may clarify exposure and access while leaving bodily processes open. The better question is not “Which lens wins?” but “What does this lens explain, what evidence supports it, and what remains unexplained?”

For Noor, night work does not rule out a bodily process. A bodily finding would not make rent, course policy, or Noor's own aims disappear. The lenses can conflict, combine, or prove irrelevant; evidence must decide.

Completion problem

Check
id check-completion-problem

A fictional learner writes: “Noor's missed seminars prove she lacks motivation.” Complete the repair:

“That sentence turns an observation into an explanation. Before choosing an explanation, I would add the ______ lens by asking ______, and I would add the ______ lens by asking ______.”

Use two different lenses. Then state one fact that could make you revise your preferred explanation.

Feedback. Many pairs can work. A person-lens question might ask what Noor wants from the course and how she understands the change. A system-lens question might ask how night work and attendance rules constrain her options. A brain-lens question might ask what health or sleep evidence is missing. Your answer is strong if each blank contains a genuine question rather than a disguised conclusion, and if the revision fact could actually alter your account.

Supported boundary problem

Mina is fictional. A student group wants to build a classroom presentation about how noise, discrimination, social support, and university procedures might affect her participation.

  1. Choose three lenses and write one question from each.
  2. Apply the scope test. Who is affected, what is the action, what are the possible consequences, and what governed route—if any—is required?
  3. Now change one detail: the group plans to interview a real classmate about private health experiences and publish the recording. Which new boundary questions arise?

Feedback. The fictional presentation can remain academic if it analyzes questions and evidence without diagnosing Mina. The changed task introduces a real person, privacy, consent, publication rights, and possible harm. “It is for education” does not answer those concerns. Revise the activity to use a fully fictional case or a purpose-commissioned contribution governed by explicit consent and rights; do not improvise with a classmate's private story.

Independent problem

Create a new fictional situation in two sentences, without borrowing from a real person's private experience. Then:

  • write one question for each of the six lenses;
  • identify the two lenses you would investigate first and explain why, without declaring the others irrelevant;
  • propose one academic task and one action that would require a separate clinical route;
  • justify both classifications with the four-question scope test.

Use this quality check: each lens should change the question being asked; each boundary decision should name the target and action; and at least one possible finding should be able to change your priorities.

Source Note
claims claim-integrated-field-model, claim-lenses-are-question-generators, claim-authority-requires-governed-practice, claim-personal-stories-require-governancesources source-psychiatry-syllabus, source-wpa-training, source-abpn-certification

These activities apply the course's six-lens synthesis and academic boundary. The syllabus also supplies the consent, privacy, and rights boundary for using a real person's story in open course material. The external sources support the field's breadth and the distinction between study and supervised professional formation; the cases, prompts, error patterns, and feedback are original teaching material rather than source-derived examples.

scenes.title: Transfer the map to a policy question
scenes.contentHtml:
Transfer the map to a policy question

A university reports that students returning after mental-health leave are less likely to complete the next term. An administrator proposes mandatory weekly symptom reports from every returning student.

The object of study is now a policy, not a fictional individual's difficulties. The six-lens map still works, but you must adapt the questions.

Check
id check-policy-transfer

Before reading the guidance, write a short policy-analysis plan that includes:

  1. at least four of the six lenses;
  2. the evidence needed before claiming the policy would help;
  3. the perspectives or goals of affected students;
  4. one possible benefit and one possible harm;
  5. a boundary sentence stating what your analysis can and cannot decide.

Guidance after your attempt

A mind-lens question could ask how the reporting requirement changes attention, fear, or willingness to seek support. A person-lens question could ask what returning students value and what routes they want. Relationship and culture questions could examine trust, stigma, language, and whose account is treated as credible. A system-lens question could examine privacy, accessibility, finances, service capacity, and the rule's effect on participation. A brain-lens question might be relevant only if a specific biological claim is actually proposed; the map does not require you to invent one.

Your evidence plan should distinguish the initial observation—lower completion—from a causal explanation. It should also include outcomes that could reveal harm, not only the outcome the administrator hopes to improve.

The work remains academic when you compare evidence, assumptions, rights, and possible system effects. It would cross into a different governed route if you used the policy exercise to assess a named student's mental state, direct that student's treatment, or claim professional authority over an individual decision.

What successful transfer looks like

You have transferred the model if you can:

  • select rather than mechanically list lenses;
  • show how a policy changes the questions each selected lens asks;
  • keep affected people's goals and system power visible;
  • identify evidence that would change your conclusion;
  • state the boundary between policy analysis and individual clinical judgment.

If your plan simply says “consider all factors,” revise it. Name the factor, the lens, the question, the evidence, and the limit.

Source Note
claims claim-integrated-field-model, claim-lenses-are-question-generators, claim-authority-requires-governed-practicesources source-psychiatry-syllabus, source-wpa-training, source-abpn-certification

The transfer task uses the course model at a policy scale. WPA supports including public mental health, culture, service systems, and lived-experience participation in psychiatric education. The task does not claim that the proposed policy is effective or lawful, and it does not authorize an individual assessment.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the field and your boundary
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the field and your boundary

Close the lesson and try to complete these sentences from memory:

  1. The six lenses are…
  2. A lens is a question, not…
  3. A several-lens account does not assume…
  4. The four scope-test questions ask…
  5. Academic learning can prepare me to…, but it does not authorize me to…

Now compare your reconstruction with this compact map:

  • Mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system keep six kinds of question available.
  • Select and weigh; do not merely accumulate. Evidence determines which lenses matter and how much.
  • Knowledge is not authority. The target, action, possible consequence, and governed route determine the boundary.
  • Stay revisable. A good account says what evidence could change it.

Return to your opening answer to “What does psychiatry study?” Revise it in one or two sentences. A defensible answer should include the field's medical identity, several levels of study, and a limit on what academic study permits you to do.

Recovery route

Mastery check and retry

Complete the four-item assessment attached to this lesson without looking back first. Use its item rubrics after the attempt. Every listed criterion is part of the target; a missed criterion is a study route, not a judgment about you.

  • If the six lenses do not come back, return to Six lenses, six kinds of question, read the lens questions once, move away from the table, and reconstruct it from memory.
  • If you list lenses but cannot weigh them, redo Noor's case using “ask whether” and name one lens that might prove irrelevant.
  • If the authority boundary is unclear, apply the four questions to Ana and Ben again, then retry with a different fictional case.
  • If policy transfer is weak, replace “consider everything” with one named question, one needed source of evidence, and one limit for each selected lens.

Retake with an equivalent fictional case or policy. Do not memorize the option order.

Accessibility and alternatives

This lesson is text-first, untimed, and does not require personal health information. Tables repeat their logic in prose. You may respond by typing, speech-to-text, a transcribed audio response, or a tutor-mediated oral explanation; the assessed operation is the structure of your reasoning, not the response medium. No distinction depends on color, sound, motion, dragging, or a timed reveal.

If the material connects with a difficult personal experience, pause or leave without penalty. EmbeddedKnowledge does not monitor learners or provide crisis care. For personal help, use an appropriately qualified local service or current emergency route rather than treating a lesson activity as care.

What comes next

Later lessons will ask you to retrieve this map while distinguishing description from inference, comparing explanatory models, examining history and power, and evaluating classification and recovery. The six lenses will become more precise. The boundary stays: academic and simulated mastery can support future supervised development, but this book does not confer clinical authority.

Source Note
claims claim-integrated-field-model, claim-lenses-are-question-generators, claim-psychiatry-medical-specialty, claim-authority-requires-governed-practicesources source-psychiatry-syllabus, source-wpa-training, source-abpn-certification

The synthesis restates the course's integrated field model and boundary. WPA and ABPN sources demonstrate that professional formation involves supervised and jurisdiction-specific arrangements beyond reading or simulation. Nothing in this lesson certifies clinical competence or effectiveness for learners.

assessment.title: Psychiatry's field and boundaries mastery check

items.prompt: Without looking back, name the six lenses, state one question each contributes, and explain why a lens is not the same thing as a cause or conclusion.

answer.correct: Mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system; each must be linked to a distinct question, and the response must explain that a lens directs inquiry while evidence determines whether and how it contributes.
answer.summary: Reconstruct all six lenses as questions, not predetermined causes.
answer.reasoning: The outcome requires a usable field map. Naming labels alone is insufficient because the learner must show what each lens helps investigate and preserve the model's limit: a lens does not prove relevance, causation, or equal weight.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent wording for mind, brain and body, person, relationships, culture, and systems is acceptable when each question remains distinct.
A response may combine brain and body in its wording if it retains the lesson's brain-lens meaning.
commonErrors: Omitting person or culture because they appear to overlap with mind or relationship.
Listing six labels without stating the question each contributes.
Treating the lenses as six equal causes or as a diagnostic checklist.

items.prompt: Which statement best uses the six-lens map when evidence is still limited?
items.stimulus: A fictional student reports poor sleep after beginning night work and moving into noisy housing.

options.text: The system lens proves that work and housing are the complete cause.
All six lenses must receive equal causal weight because psychiatry is integrative.
Work and housing generate system questions; other lenses remain available if evidence makes them relevant, and none is yet a conclusion.
Because psychiatry is medical, only a brain-level question is legitimate.

answer.summary: Use lenses to generate bounded questions whose relevance and weight remain evidence-dependent.
answer.reasoning: The correct statement uses known context to ask a system-level question while preserving uncertainty. The other options convert a lens into proof, require equal causal shares, or exclude non-brain evidence by definition.
commonErrors: Choosing a single complete cause because it is concrete.
Confusing medical identity with brain-only explanation.

items.prompt: Select every action that remains inside this course's academic or simulated learning boundary.

options.text: Compare two published explanations and identify what evidence would distinguish them.
Tell a real relative which psychiatric disorder explains their recent behaviour.
Build a six-lens question map for a clearly fictional case.
Advise a real classmate to stop a prescribed medicine.
Analyze possible benefits, harms, and evidence needs for a fictional service policy.

answer.summary: Evidence comparison, fictional-case mapping, and policy analysis are academic; diagnosing or directing treatment for a real person is not.
answer.reasoning: The scope test focuses on the affected person and proposed action. Academic comparison, fictional reasoning, and fictional policy analysis produce inspectable arguments. The excluded actions apply a diagnosis or treatment direction to real people and therefore require evidence, supervision, and clinical authority outside this book.
commonErrors: Selecting a real-person action because the learner intends to help.
Rejecting policy analysis merely because it concerns mental health.
Assuming advanced academic knowledge itself grants authority.

items.prompt: Write an academic analysis plan for the proposed policy. Select at least four lenses, state one question from each, name evidence that could change your conclusion, and finish with a boundary sentence explaining what the analysis cannot decide about an individual student.
items.stimulus: A university observes lower next-term completion among students returning from mental-health leave and proposes mandatory weekly symptom reports for every returning student.

answer.correct: A complete response selects at least four relevant lenses, turns each into a policy-level question, names disconfirming or decision-changing evidence, includes affected students' goals and possible harms, and states that the analysis cannot assess, diagnose, or direct treatment for an individual student.
answer.summary: Adapt the six-lens map to a system decision while preserving evidence and clinical boundaries.
answer.reasoning: Transfer requires selection and adaptation rather than copying the Noor example. The policy makes system, person, mind, relationship, and culture questions especially plausible; a brain question is optional unless a biological claim is proposed. The final boundary must keep policy analysis separate from individual clinical judgment.
acceptableResponses: Different sets of four or more lenses are acceptable when each is justified and not mechanically included.
A learner may challenge the initial completion measure as part of the evidence plan.
commonErrors: Writing only 'consider all factors' without lens-specific questions.
Assuming the observed association proves the proposed policy will help.
Ignoring privacy, accessibility, autonomy, or unintended harms.
Using the policy analysis to make a judgment about a named student's mental state or treatment.

criteria.description: Names mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system without collapsing distinct lenses.
States a distinct and accurate question for each lens.
Explains that a lens directs inquiry but does not establish relevance, causation, or equal weight.
Selects the response that preserves system questions, other possible lenses, and uncertainty.
Selects evidence comparison, fictional-case mapping, and fictional policy analysis.
Excludes diagnosing a real relative and directing a real classmate's medicine change.
Selects and justifies at least four relevant lenses rather than listing them mechanically.
Turns each selected lens into a concrete policy-level question and includes affected students' goals or perspectives.
Names evidence, benefits, or harms that could change the conclusion.
States that the analysis cannot assess, diagnose, or direct treatment for an individual student.

sources.title: Psychiatry: Mind, Brain, Person, Society

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson retains and adapts the syllabus's named mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system framing plus its explicit academic-versus-clinical boundary. ATTRIBUTION.md identifies the exact commit, CC BY 4.0 licence, retained material, and modifications. The lesson adds original cases, explanation, scope test, practice, transfer, and assessment.

agentAccess.notes: Read from the authorized local repository checkout. The exact commit's content licence permits adaptation with attribution and change notice; the public site robots file allows access, and no source-specific restriction on this agent use was found.

sources.title: High Quality Post-Graduate Training in Psychiatry

sources.locator: Pages 1–4: cultural context and lived experience; recommendations 8–19 on local systems, supervised development, public mental health, research, service-user involvement, and training variation
sources.license: No open licence displayed in the checked PDF; citation-only factual reference

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only bounded facts about the stated breadth, supervision, and international variation of postgraduate psychiatry training were used. No wording, table, recommendation text, case, graphic, or document structure was copied or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The public robots file allowed general access subject to a ten-second crawl delay. A check of public site routes and the source page found no term prohibiting this single, non-bulk agent access; no login, paywall, rate limit, or technical control was bypassed. This is a dated observation, not a warranty.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only facts establishing psychiatry's medical-specialty framing and one jurisdiction's separate physician-training, licensure, and certification route were used. No source wording, examination content, tables, media, or page structure was copied or adapted.

Copyright restrictions were honored by facts-only research and original expression; no login or technical control was bypassed.

claims.statement: This course studies psychiatry through mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system rather than reducing the field to a single scale.
claims.scope: An EmbeddedKnowledge curricular framing informed by international training breadth and a current United States medical-specialty example; not a universal philosophical definition of psychiatry.
claims.uncertainty: Professional and intellectual traditions weight these levels differently, and the model has not yet received lesson-level independent review.

claims.statement: The lesson's six lenses generate distinct questions and do not imply that all six are causal, relevant, compatible, or equally weighted in every situation.
claims.scope: A declared use rule for the course's six-lens curricular model.
claims.uncertainty: This model organizes questions; it does not validate a causal explanation or replace a discipline-specific method.

claims.statement: The World Psychiatric Association position statement approved in 2023 places culture, public mental health, research, service systems, lived-experience participation, and supervised development within postgraduate psychiatry training alongside clinical content.
claims.scope: The content of WPA Position Statement GA 23.10.3, approved in 2023; a professional position statement rather than an empirical estimate or universally binding curriculum.
claims.uncertainty: The document header states a 2017 last-review date despite 2023 approval, and later replacement should be checked before publication updates.

claims.statement: A United States board source places psychiatry within medicine and describes the specialty as connecting biological, psychological, and social domains.
claims.scope: The American Board of Psychiatry and Neurology's 2026 public certification page; used as a United States example, not a globally sufficient definition.
claims.uncertainty: Other jurisdictions and psychiatric traditions may articulate the specialty's scope differently.

claims.statement: Academic reading, fictional-case reasoning, and simulation do not by themselves establish clinical authority; supervised patient care, competency decisions, and jurisdiction-specific authorization belong to separate governed routes.
claims.scope: The EmbeddedKnowledge course boundary, supported by a global postgraduate-training statement and a current United States certification example; it does not enumerate every jurisdiction's legal requirements.
claims.uncertainty: The exact protected activities, titles, supervisory structures, and authorization requirements vary by jurisdiction.

claims.statement: The WPA position statement reports cross-country variation in the level, duration, quality, and supervision of psychiatry training and requires local systems and regulations to shape curricula; ABPN documents one current United States certification and licensure route.
claims.scope: Cross-country training variation described by WPA, with ABPN used only to instantiate one United States certification and licensure route.
claims.uncertainty: No comparison of certification or legal authorization across multiple jurisdictions is claimed.

claims.statement: Using a real person's private health story in openly published learning material requires an explicit consent, privacy, and rights process; describing the use as educational is not sufficient by itself.
claims.scope: The EmbeddedKnowledge case and lived-experience contribution standard for open CC BY 4.0 course material; not a complete statement of privacy or consent law in any jurisdiction.
claims.uncertainty: A future purpose-commissioned contribution would also require review under the then-current repository and local legal requirements.

terms.definition: A medical field that studies and responds to patterns of experience, behaviour, bodily process, relationship, culture, and context; this lesson teaches its academic scope, not authority to practise it.
A disciplined level or perspective used to generate a particular kind of question; a lens is not evidence that its proposed explanation is relevant or causal.
Study that builds knowledge and reasoning through sources, fictional cases, analyses, and simulations without conferring authority to assess or treat real people.
A designed practice setting using a fictional scenario, standardized participant, model, or supplied data so a learner can rehearse reasoning without representing the activity as real-patient care.
Jurisdiction-dependent responsibility and authorization to perform clinical actions with real people within a regulated professional role; academic knowledge alone does not create it.
The lesson's four-question procedure for checking who is affected, what action is proposed, what could happen if it is wrong, and which governed route the action requires.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson authorship and licence

Welcome to Psychiatry: Mind, Brain, Person, Society (PSY-WEL-001), version 0.1.0, was prepared for EmbeddedKnowledge under the accountable principal VSBDev (github:VSBDev). Except for the separately identified source material below, the lesson prose, fictional cases, tables, prompts, assessment, glossary, and structured records are offered under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International licence.

No third-party image, audio, video, software, dataset, patient record, diagnostic-manual text, rating-scale item, or clinical case is included. Noor, Mina, Ana, Ben, Chen, Dev, and all situations in the lesson are wholly fictional and were created for this pack.

Adapted curriculum source

This lesson adapts EmbeddedKnowledge, Psychiatry: Mind, Brain, Person, Society, curriculum version 0.2 (23 July 2026) (source-psychiatry-syllabus), preserved at immutable commit 951fdc24c7aa209b217da4004f3e91ebd54862ec in the EmbeddedKnowledge repository and licensed there under CC BY 4.0.

Material retained and adapted: the named mind, brain, person, relationship, culture, and system framing; the distinction between academic or simulated learning and supervised clinical authority; and the three learner routes.

Modifications: the syllabus material was reorganized and rewritten as an original 90-minute, six-scene welcome lesson. The lesson adds the fictional Noor anchor, the six-question teaching table, a four-question scope test, a complete worked boundary decision, shortcut repair, fading practice, policy transfer, retrieval and recovery routes, accessibility alternatives, a glossary, and a four-item assessment. No syllabus case, question, table, assessment, or media was copied.

Citation-only factual sources

The World Psychiatric Association position statement (source-wpa-training) was used only for bounded facts about the stated breadth, cultural and systems context, supervision, and international variation of postgraduate psychiatry training. The American Board of Psychiatry and Neurology page (source-abpn-certification) was used only for facts about psychiatry's medical-specialty framing and one current United States certification route. Both sources remain under their publishers' terms. No wording, recommendation text, table, examination content, media, or document structure was copied or adapted.

Agent assistance and provenance

Codex materially researched, designed, and authored the candidate under the accountability of VSBDev with this disclosed provenance:

  • System: Codex
  • Provider: OpenAI
  • Model: GPT-5
  • Version: Codex CLI 0.144.6
  • Run ID: author-codex-gpt5-20260723-87a40bc4-f444-4eba-86ba-ac61945b672c
  • Material-instructions digest: sha256:168dbbb143e073973d17ff35cfffbcbfcd3f1c1a5e78f83a18c1933b6d019d8c

The model name is the model identity exposed to this authoring run. The SHA-256 digest covers the exact discloseable MATERIAL INSTRUCTIONS file used for this lesson. It does not claim to cover hidden provider or system instructions that were not exportable.

Candidate status

This is an authoring candidate. Its lesson status and source confidence remain draft and pending-review; all claims remain pending-review. No review or adjudication artifact exists. Validation or self-audit does not establish approval, publication, outcome coverage, learner effectiveness, university credit, clinical competence, licensure, board eligibility, or authority to practise psychiatry.
